headline: Tom’s answers show a performance pattern of staying safe: he ships, but he can’t yet name Principal-level leverage or cross-team ownership clearly.
summary_bullets: Tom frames the core gap as leverage and Principal scope: “this team's bar still feels fuzzy” and he “stayed in my lane instead of forcing cross-team decisions.”
He links his caution to trust and visibility: “I have not built enough trust with adjacent teams yet” and the gap is “visible ownership,” not technical ability.
His next-commitment question is skipped, leaving no concrete plan to close the expectation-alignment and stakeholder-trust gaps he named.
understanding_paragraph: Tom understands his execution is steady, but he cannot yet articulate what “Principal” means on this specific team. He avoids cross-team decisions and tradeoff ownership until he has explicit expectations and adjacent-team trust. The session revealed that his current operating model is constrained by unclear bars and low visible ownership, not by technical capability.

axes.meaning: Weak signal on wellbeing; not actionable from this session alone.
He ships but under-aims at leverage; worth watching for continued safe execution.
Defining signal: Principal scope and team bar are unclear, driving lane-staying and delayed tradeoffs.
Real pattern: growth is blocked by trust/ownership gaps and delayed feedback calibration.

brutal_truth_employee: Tom’s read is blunt: “I am shipping, but I am not sure I am aiming at the highest-leverage work yet.” He also admits the mechanism: “I have mostly stayed in my lane” and “I have not built enough trust with adjacent teams yet.”
brutal_truth_manager: When Tom said “this team's bar still feels fuzzy,” the conversation stayed at diagnosis level instead of forcing a concrete Principal bar definition and examples from the next cycle. The biggest missed moment is the skipped “first concrete change” answer—he left without committing to a specific scope/decision artifact.

next_actions.action: Schedule a 30-minute calibration with Tom: define “Principal impact” for this team using 2 recent examples and 3 measurable behaviors.
Assign Tom one cross-team ownership decision for next cycle, with a named stakeholder and an explicit expectation-setting checklist to use before he executes.
Re-ask for a concrete commitment: “What decision or project will you own end-to-end next cycle, and what feedback will you request by week one?”

watch_for: Before next 1:1: Tom can name the team’s Principal bar in concrete terms and tie it to one upcoming decision, not “fuzzy” language.
Within two cycles: adjacent teams start giving earlier, sharper feedback that Tom can cite; if not, the trust/ownership gap is persisting.
